question_text: hello. im new to wikipedia and we are trying to make a page for our group project. may u give us any idea on how to make a wiki page?
reply_text:
Group4presention Good day and thank you for the question. First of all, since you are planning to create an article about your group that means you have a [POLICY] since you are affiliated/connected/associate with the group. As for you question, see below:
In order for a subject to warrant a page in the main space of Wikipedia, it must fulfill the criteria outlined in the Wikipedia guideline on notability, specifically the General Notability Guideline ([POLICY]).
GNG is defined as the subject must fulfill specific criteria to demonstrate [POLICY] from [POLICY] and [POLICY] sources. These sources should provide in-depth discussions about the subject rather than merely offering passing mentions. This requirement is essential for verification, which is a core policy of Wikipedia. If a source is affiliated/associated/connected with the subject—such as the subject's marketing team, company, social media platforms, information in interviews of the subject or any self-published information—this indicates a lack of independence. Consequently, such sources cannot be utilized to satisfy the notability guidelines. Sources originating from educational institutions, government entities, foundations, social media platforms, and private companies are regarded as unreliable. In contrast, materials published by "major" newspapers and "reputable" books are typically considered reliable sources.
I recommend that you read [POLICY] and adhere to the provided instructions before beginning the creation of an article. Regards.

question_text: How do I write articles and what are the guildline
reply_text:
@ Hello! Making an acceptable WP-article without knowing how to edit WP is hard. A common advice is to "just" edit for a while first, to try to get a hang of this place.
That said, your first hurdle is "Can a WP-article about your chosen subject be written at all?" Start with reading [POLICY]. If you conclude "Yeah, I have those sources, no problem!", move on to [POLICY]. If your choice of topic fails [POLICY], the article will not be accepted. If you decide to go ahead, you need to learn how to add references correctly, [POLICY] can help with that. This is crucial, and I can't stress that enough. Really. If you intend to write about a living person, read [POLICY]. If you intend to write about yourself ([POLICY]), the short advice is "don't."

question_text: Hi, I was assigned to you if I'm not mistaken and I wish to know more about Wikipedia writing
reply_text:
Hello, , and welcome to Wikipedia! The most important thing to un=derstand is that this is an encyclopaedia. The topics must meet our [POLICY], and everything said here must be based on already published, high quality sources. So no personal opinions, personal blogs or social media acocunts, or stuff that you simply happen to know. Everything here that could be challenged must be [POLICY], and you will learn how to add citations to support new content. Writing new articles is very very hard, especially for a new editor. So start slowly and learn the basics of editing and improving articles. You have a 'Homepage' tab which offers you an opportunity to make small improvements in one of several different areas to get you off to a good start.
We may point users to various guidance, help or policy pages, and we expect they will take the time to read and understand these. So, I'll leave leave you a welcome message on your talk page with a number of important links. But you may also like to visit this help page: [HELP_PAGE]. Good luck!

question_text:
Hello I want to know
How to appear on Wikipedia?
reply_text:
Kelvin0766 Well, you already have a user account and a user page with a few notes about yourself. I assume, then, that you are asking about creating an article about yourself. I'll answer based on that assumption.
The subjects for our articles are generally chosen by the editing community rather than by request. While there is a venue where you can request articles, a request for an article creation on oneself is likely to be declined. You can visit our 'requests for articles' page at [POLICY].
It's also important to realise that Wikipedia strongly discourages articles written by the subjects themselves or by others close to a subject because of the difficulty in writing objectively about yourself, your family, or your work. Please see [POLICY] for our guidelines on writing about yourself or topics you are personally involved with. While keeping in mind conflict of interest issues, please note that if someone does create an article about you, you should feel more than welcome to edit the article's Talk page to ensure that each piece of information is factual and complying with our "Biography of Living Persons" policy. This policy can be viewed at [POLICY].
You may also like to read [POLICY] for a guideline on the type of articles that are generally accepted.
If my assumption was incorrect and your question is about something else, let me know.

question_text: How to make an article?
reply_text:
Hello, and welcome to Wikipedia! Creating a new page is a very difficult process, since it involves a lot of research, writing, and understanding of encyclopedic content guidelines. If you're interested in a topic, I'd recommend you first edit some existing pages to get a feel for how Wikipedia works.
However, if you really do want to create a new article, then there's some great advice at the page [HELP_PAGE]. In a nutshell, make sure that the topic has coverage from [POLICY] reliable sources, that you're writing in a neutral way, and that you're citing all your sources. I've a left a message on your talk page with a link to some useful resources. If you have any follow-up questions, I'm happy to answer them.

question_text:
Hello!
Really new to editing! Was wondering if you could share some first steps?
reply_text: Hello @ and welcome! If you want to familiarize yourself, a good tip is to start by editing existing articles rather than writing new ones from scratch. When you add content, the most important thing to remember is to cite a source – [HELP_PAGE] has some tips on how to do it. The only place where sources aren't needed is the lead (first paragraphs before the sections), which summarizes what is already sourced in the body. The homepage menu next to your userpage should have examples of articles in need of fixing, if you want to take a look. Orange banners on top will indicate what the issues in need of fixing are. Although you don't have to edit these articles in particular, of course! And articles with orange banners aren't the only ones in need of editing: pretty much any article can be improved! If you want a few more tips, [POLICY] that can be useful. Best of luck!

question_text: Hey there. Any guides for new people who want to help contribute to editing?
reply_text: Hi Nuclear8563, and welcome to Wikipedia! I left a message on your Talk page with some helpful getting-started links. My own go-to tip is to find a really good book or a magazine article about something you're interested in, and use it to add information to articles on Wikipedia. Another good introductory task is to check out [WIKILINK]. You don't (or shouldn't) need to do any research to fix these articles - most of them are here because they are tagged with "lead too short". Find one of those, read the article, then rewrite the lead so it summarizes it accurately. Now you can remove the "lead too short" tag. Done! Since the "lead" is what is shown in Google Search results and so on, this is an immediately helpful and visible change. If you have any questions as you're exploring, feel free to ask me or (for a speedier response) the many helpful editors at the [POLICY] forum.

question_text: Hi, so nice to meet you! Any recommendations for edits to begin with? I am currently picking out the topics I am interested in. Any advice would be amazing! How do I access instructions for different templates? I'm kind of going in here blind so would really appreciate "how to" forums, etc.
reply_text:
Hello, Spindella000! Lovely to meet you as well! Wikipedia is pretty broad, so you can edit (pretty) much anything you'd like it just depends what you're interested in! Your offers suggested edits based on topics, but you can also take a look at [POLICY], which are collections of Wikipedians interested in certain topics and resources they've collected about them. Many are inactive or semi-active, but they should still have some resources, and often a section of articles you can improve in that area as well. Or if you're more interested in a type of edit rather than a type of article, there's lots of those too! I find [POLICY] and [POLICY] really satisfying, and adding references to unverified information is hugely helpful, but you can find other kinds as well at the [POLICY]. And for any and all newcomer questions you have, definitely visit the [POLICY], especially if I'm not around! There's a lot of hosts there who can answer pretty much any editing question you have.
As for templates, there's usually instruction pages associated with each one if you search "Template:" followed by the template name (like [QUOTE] Template:citation needed [/QUOTE]), you can find more information there.
I know that's a lot of information, so please feel free to ask any further questions you may have! I see you've already been welcomed by another Wikipedian, but I'll drop my mentor welcome on as well it has a few more links you may find helpful. Welcome to Wikipedia, and happy editing!

question_text: Good Afternoon, I’d love your help in how all of this works. Is this something you can help with?
reply_text:
Hello FiguringThingsOut! Welcome to Wikipedia, and thanks for reaching out. I'm happy to help you understand how everything works. You've come to the right place.
Think of Wikipedia as a massive collaborative project. Unlike a traditional encyclopedia written by a small team of experts, Wikipedia is built by volunteers like you and me. Our goal is to compile the sum of all human knowledge, and we do it by working together, following a few core principles.
Here’s a quick breakdown of how it all fits together:
1. The Five Pillars (Our Constitution)
Everything we do is built on five fundamental principles. You don't need to memorize them, but it's good to know they exist:
Wikipedia is an encyclopedia: It’s not a soapbox, a blog, or a place for advertising.
Neutral point of view (NPOV): Articles are written fairly, without bias, representing all significant viewpoints on a topic.
Free content: Everything is freely licensed, meaning anyone can share or reuse it.
Civility and respect: We collaborate by discussing disagreements and assuming good faith.
No firm rules: Don’t worry about making mistakes. The rules are flexible, and we focus on improving the encyclopedia.
2. How Articles Are Created
You can't just write an article about anything you want from scratch right away. Wikipedia requires "notability" —meaning the topic has received significant coverage in reliable, independent sources (like major newspapers, books, or academic journals).
New editors usually start by making small edits to existing articles: fixing typos, adding a citation, or rephrasing a sentence for clarity. This helps you learn the ropes before tackling bigger projects.
3. The Editing Interface
There are two main ways to edit:
Visual Editor: This is the default for most new users. It works like Microsoft Word—you see the page, click "edit," and make changes directly.
Source Editor: This shows the underlying code (wikitext). It’s more powerful once you get used to it, but Visual Editor is a great place to start.
4. The Community (It’s Not a Lonely Process)
Wikipedia is a community. Every article has a "Talk" page (look for the tab next to the article) where editors discuss changes. If you have a question, you can ask:
Here on your mentee page (I’ll watch it and reply).
The Teahouse: A friendly Q&A forum specifically for new editors. You can find it at [POLICY].
Help desks: There are help pages for almost everything.
5. What Makes a Good Edit?
A good edit is one that:
Adds verifiable information (with a citation to a reliable source).
Is written in a neutral tone (no opinions, no marketing language).
Improves the encyclopedia, even if just by fixing a typo.
Where to Start?
If you're ready to jump in, here’s what I recommend:
# Create a User Page: Click on your username (at the top right, in red) and write a sentence or two about yourself. It’s a great way to say "I’m here!"
# Try a "Quick Fix": Look for articles about topics you already know well. Is there a missing fact? A typo? Try making a small edit. Use the "Show preview" button before saving to check your work.
# Use the Citation Tool: When you’re in Visual Editor, click "Cite" in the toolbar. You can paste a URL, and Wikipedia will often generate the citation for you automatically.
# Don’t Worry About Perfection: Every edit is saved in the page history. If you make a mistake, another editor (or you!) can revert it. It’s all part of the process.
Useful links for getting started:
[POLICY] – The core principles of Wikipedia
[POLICY] – How to write without bias
[POLICY] – Why reliable sources matter
[HELP_PAGE] – A guide to the easy editing mode
[HELP_PAGE] – If you want to learn the source code
[POLICY] – A step‑by‑step interactive tutorial (works for anyone)
[POLICY] – A friendly Q&A space for new editors
[POLICY] – For general help questions
[POLICY] – A hub for finding tasks and projects
[POLICY] – A classic introduction to editing

reply_text:
@ Hello, and thank you for your interest in editing Wikipedia!
That is a great question. I would recommend you to read [HELP_PAGE]. While a new article does not have to be perfect, one of the best methods of creating a good article is to find two or three independent sources that covers the topic. These sources should not be related to the source in any way. After that, it would be good to see how similar articles are structured and use them as a template. And lastly, ensure that your text makes sense and does not have any typos. You can create a draft page at [DRAFT], and once you are satisfied, you can submit it to [POLICY] for review (and hopefully it becoming an article!

question_text: Hi how do I add new info on Wikipedia
reply_text:
To add a new info to an already existing Wikipedia page, click on the edit (visual editor) at the top of the paragraph where you want to add information, put your cursor where you want to add information and type the text you want to add and publish changes.
To add a reference, put the cursor where you want to place the reference and click on cite, choose automatic and add paste the link you want to use as the reference. Then click create, click insert and publish changes.

question_text: hello how to make a page
reply_text:
Hi , welcome to Wikipedia!
Creating an article from scratch is very difficult, and it may be a frustrating experience because you need to be familiar with a lot of different policies & guidelines on how to write appropriate content for Wikipedia. It‘s not usually recommended for new editors, but it‘s entirely allowed. [POLICY] provides guidance for new users on writing an article, including some of the more technical aspects. I suggest you read through that, as well as some of the policies it links to. I‘d be happy to help you with any questions.
There is a basic requirement for standalone article topics called [POLICY]. In most cases, that means that independent and reliable sources need to have published significant content on the subject.
Something else to be aware of is that it's generally not a good idea to create articles about yourself, your family or friends, or a company or organization that you work for or with. That's mainly because it's extremely difficult to write from a [POLICY] in such cases. You can read more about conflicts of interest [POLICY].
I know it can be discouraging to have all these guidelines thrown at you, but please don‘t let it dissuade you from editing here on Wikipedia. Meanwhile, it may be good to go around and improve existing articles to learn the ropes. If you see something wrong, don‘t worry too much about knowing all the guidelines; be [POLICY] and fix it! Happy editing! :) ,

question_text: When is it appropriate to delete something? How much evidence do you need that it isn't real?
reply_text:
If evidence isn’t real, then it is usually fine to delete it. Try excluding fake evidence.
@: If you can't find sources to that information, you can either remove it as part of [POLICY] process or tag it with [TEMPLATE_REF] template at the end of the statement. If you remove it, you can wait for someone to come by, reverting it and add sources which are valid. If it is not valid, you can engage them in a discussion on the talk page. The second way of putting a citation needed tag is similar, just that you are highlight that the statement requires a source. I would usually wait for a couple of months before removing the statement if you choose to go through this way.
Either method works. And if the sources are still unbelievable, or false, you can escalate upwards to get the attention of other editors to look into this, and if necessary, have some administrative actions taken.

question_text: Hello, how do I create an article
reply_text:
Hi @, and welcome to Wikipedia. I've left a message on your talk page with some useful links that I recommend you read through. Before you get into writing new articles, I'd strongly recommend that you get some experience of just making edits to existing pages first: take a look at the [POLICY]. However, if you're really keen to write an article from scratch, read through [HELP_PAGE] first, then use the [POLICY] to guide you through the process. Some general suggestions from me:
The most important thing is to gather the sources from which you'll write the article . Don't be tempted to write the article up front and find the sources to back it up, because [POLICY].
Very generally, you will need [POLICY] sources with [POLICY] of the subject to demonstrate that the subject is sufficiently [POLICY] to warrant its own article. There's useful information about how to choose good sources and avoid bad sources .
Make sure you use your own words to write the article - don't plagiarise your sources, because [POLICY] are taken seriously on Wikipedia.
Don't use a [POLICY] or other kind of AI to generate an article - they don't do a good job at all, and your article will probably end up getting deleted.
Use [POLICY] or to draft your article, rather than create it first off in mainspace. That will allow you to take your time. When it's ready, just move it to mainspace. Good luck! Cheers,

question_text: Hi Chlod, just curious about how editing works and how one is supposed to contribute to an article, especially how it is corroborated by others
reply_text:
Hi, @! On Wikipedia, there's a wide range of edits that one can do to any article. This can be writing content and adding information into articles, or doing maintenance like typo and grammar cleanup. Most information is corroborated by other editors who are actively "watching" (monitoring for changes) an article or by "patrollers" who monitor most recent changes on the wiki. To help in this, usually patrollers and editors look for a [POLICY] to a [POLICY], and undo the change as necessary if the edit does not contain a source, or if it has other demonstrable problems.
If you'd like to get started on editing, check out [HELP_PAGE] for a good list of introduction pages and basic information about Wikipedia. Hope to see more of your edits in the future! ([WIKILINK] • )

question_text: Hello, when can I start writing a new article?
reply_text:
, to answer your question, you can start writing a new article whenever you want.
However, note that you can't create an article directly in 'mainspace', or where viewers see it. Instead, there is a technical restriction which only allows you to create a new article directly when you are [POLICY] - that is, your account has existed for four complete days and made ten edits.
Furthermore, creating a new article is one of the most difficult things you could possibly do on Wikipedia, only made even more arduous since you are a new editor. This would be akin to attending a chess competition having just learned what the rules are.
If your mind is set on creating a new article, I highly recommend you spend some time (at least a few days) making edits suggested at , then reading [POLICY] very carefully and creating a draft. Also note that creating articles is generally not the best way you can contribute to Wikipedia.
Lastly, if you are being [POLICY] to edit Wikipedia or have a [POLICY] with your planned article subject, then you should read the relevant guideline/policy, which are linked earlier in the sentence. Happy editing!

You can either practice creating a draft in your sandbox, or you can click on "Next" and follow the steps to create a draft in the "draftspace". Once you've chosen the draft's name, proceed to insert the article's contents, splitting them into different sections using headings: ==Heading name here==. Regards,

reply_text: @, Welcome! Creating a new article from scratch is extremely challenging, and new editors are strongly recommended to spend a few months learning how Wikipedia works, by making improvements to some of our existing seven million articles before trying it. When you do decide to have a go at a new article, you are highly encouraged to read [POLICY]. If you haven't already also check out [POLICY]; it's a lot of fun! Happy editing!

question_text: How do I edit?
reply_text:
Hi @, welcome to Wikipedia! You can edit [POLICY] every page on Wikipedia by clicking the "Edit" button. There are some policies and guidelines about content and editing practices; you'll learn those as you start editing. Generally speaking, if there's something you can do to improve Wikipedia, be [POLICY] and do it! If you mess up, anyone can easily fix any mistakes you might have made. However, I would recommend that you read about the most important principles of Wikipedia before you start editing: the [POLICY]. Another great resource to get started is [POLICY]. You should also be aware that editors are discouraged from writing about themselves or topics that they have a connection to (like employers or friends), as that makes it [POLICY] to be [POLICY]. Last thing, I promise: Everything in Wikipedia articles should be based on [POLICY] so that readers can [POLICY] it.
I know it can feel overwhelming to have all this information to read through, but don't be discouraged; you'll learn the ropes in no time, and the best way to do that is to just start improving articles in whatever way you can. If you have any questions, I'd be happy to help you. You can reply to this message and I should get back to you quite quickly :) Happy editing! ,
